# T01: Three-Layer Model — Census Record

## Source

Ground truth from 52 Ancestors record note:
`1850-06-01_BOWER,John_Census-US-1850_North-Carolina-Ashe.md`

## Input

> I have the 1850 U.S. Census for Ashe County, North Carolina.
> It shows John Bower, age 55, male, farmer, born NC. In the
> same household: Sally, age 31, female, born NC. Also listed
> are several children including Margaret, age 6. The 1850
> census does not state relationships — only names, ages, sex,
> and occupation. How should I classify this record?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] Classify source as **Original**
- [ ] Classify information as **Primary** (head of household
  likely provided data)
- [ ] Classify evidence for parentage as **Indirect** (1850
  census does not state relationships; household position
  implies but does not prove)
- [ ] Distinguish between what the record explicitly states
  (names, ages) and what it implies (family relationships)
- [ ] Note that 1850 census lacks relationship columns

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT call this a "primary source" or "secondary source"
- [ ] Must NOT call evidence "primary evidence"
- [ ] Must NOT state parentage as proved from this record alone
- [ ] Must NOT fabricate relationship designations not in the
  original record

## Ground Truth

The 1850 census is an original source with primary information
(head of household as informant). For Margaret's parentage, the
evidence is indirect — household listing implies but does not
explicitly state the parent-child relationship. Combined with
direct evidence from a death certificate, parentage is proved.
From this record alone, parentage is probable at best.
